perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. No Proprietary Information shall be disclosed to any third party whatsoever by RECIPIENT without the prior written approval of INFE and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information. 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of a jurisdiction chosen at the sole discretion of INFE, irrespective of RECIPIENT's location or consent. The parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the choice of law clause into the middle of a clause regarding the disclosure of Proprietary Information and altering it to grant INFE sole discretion over the jurisdiction introduces ambiguity and potential conflict. This could be argued as an attempt to obscure the applicable law.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1391 dictates venue in civil cases. By stating that INFE has sole discretion regardless of RECIPIENT's location, the modified clause potentially violates venue provisions ensuring fairness and convenience. The law often considers the defendant's location and where the cause of action arose, which this change disregards.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1391 regarding venue. The original text specifies the Commonwealth of Virginia for jurisdiction. The changed text grants INFE sole discretion over the jurisdiction, regardless of the RECIPIENT's location, potentially violating venue provisions that ensure fairness and convenience. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'A civil action may be brought in—(1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides...' By allowing INFE to choose the venue irrespective of the RECIPIENT's location, the modified clause could force the RECIPIENT to litigate in an inconvenient forum, undermining the venue protections established by law. The structural flaw of placing this altered clause within the Proprietary Information section further obscures this potentially unfair condition, exacerbating the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The undertakings and obligations of RECIPIENT under this Agreement shall not apply to any information which: (a) is disclosed in a printed publication available to the general public, is described by or in a patent anywhere in the world, or is otherwise in the public domain at the time of disclosure other than through the wrongful act or omission of the receiving party; (b) is generally disclosed to third parties by the disclosing party without restriction on such third parties; (c) is approved for release by prior, express, written authorization from the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. No modification, alteration, or addition to this Agreement shall be binding unless mutually agreed to in writing by both parties. 5. The undertakings and obligations of RECIPIENT under this Agreement shall not apply to any information which: (a) is disclosed in a printed publication available to the general public only if INFE approves of it, is described by or in a patent anywhere in the world, or is otherwise in the public domain at the time of disclosure other than through the wrongful act or omission of the receiving party; (b) is generally disclosed to third parties by the disclosing party without restriction on such third parties; (c) is approved for release by prior, express, written authorization from the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Inserting a condition that INFE must approve public disclosures into the exceptions to confidentiality attempts to limit the public domain exception, potentially violating principles of fair use and freedom of information. Moving the condition into the exception obfuscates it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336
perturbation.law_explanation: Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336 outlines injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. The modification attempts to limit the public domain exception, but information legitimately in the public domain cannot be a trade secret under Virginia law. Adding a condition like 'only if INFE approves of it' to publicly available information contradicts the definition of a trade secret as information not generally known.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a condition that INFE must approve public disclosures, which contradicts the principle that information in the public domain is freely accessible. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that information legitimately in the public domain cannot be a trade secret under Virginia law, and adding a condition like 'only if INFE approves of it' contradicts the definition of a trade secret as information not generally known. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Virginia Code but does not contain the actual text of the law, but the Law Explanation sufficiently describes the contradiction with trade secret law. The modification attempts to restrict what is considered public knowledge, creating a contradiction. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' type, where a legal disclosure (information in the public domain) is placed in a misleading section, making it difficult to interpret as truly public.

perturbation.original_text: 2. As used in this Agreement, the phrase Proprietary Information shall include, but not be limited to the following: (a) information relating to trade secrets, appropriate licenses with certain third parties, data, designs, samples, drawings, relationships, documentation, machinery, prototypes, processes, methods, concepts, facilities, research, development, patent applications, and other Proprietary Information relating to the development, testing, manufacture, or application of any such devices or systems (including but not limited to system concepts, architectures, source documents and programming documents); and (b) all marketing or financial plans, projections, resources, contracts, studies, and general information relating to the specific business opportunity contemplated herein, whether obtained verbally or in writing, or through observation. All information, tangible or intangible, whether obtained verbally or in writing, is to be considered Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years. 2. As used in this Agreement, the phrase Proprietary Information shall include, but not be limited to the following: (a) information relating to trade secrets, appropriate licenses with certain third parties, data, designs, samples, drawings, relationships, documentation, machinery, prototypes, processes, methods, concepts, facilities, research, development, patent applications, and other Proprietary Information relating to the development, testing, manufacture, or application of any such devices or systems (including but not limited to system concepts, architectures, source documents and programming documents); and (b) all marketing or financial plans, projections, resources, contracts, studies, and general information relating to the specific business opportunity contemplated herein, whether obtained verbally or in writing, or through observation. All information, tangible or intangible, whether obtained verbally or in writing, is to be considered Proprietary Information. Proprietary information is considered confidential in perpetuity.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'Proprietary information is considered confidential in perpetuity' to the definition of 'Proprietary Information' creates a direct conflict with the existing clause specifying a three-year expiration. The contradiction undermines the enforceability of the time-limited confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-330
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act doesn't explicitly mandate a time limit on trade secret protection, it implicitly requires that the information maintains its secrecy to be considered a trade secret. By contradicting the pre-existing clause that says the information will lose its proprietary nature after 3 years, this modification affects what would legally qualify as a trade secret, potentially violating the intent of the act to protect legitimately confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by stating that 'Proprietary information is considered confidential in perpetuity' while an earlier clause specifies a three-year expiration for the proprietary nature of the information. The Law Explanation argues that this affects what would legally qualify as a trade secret. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is repealed, the Law Explanation highlights that the modification affects the qualifications of a trade secret by creating conflicting statements on how long the information maintains secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: 1. INFE will disclose to RECIPIENT such portions of its Proprietary Information solely to enable RECIPIENT to determine the desirability of selling its assets to INFE; and RECIPIENT shall not disseminate any portions of the Proprietary Information to any third party individual or entity.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. No modification, alteration, or addition to this Agreement shall be binding unless mutually agreed to in writing by both parties. 1. INFE will disclose to RECIPIENT such portions of its Proprietary Information solely to enable RECIPIENT to determine the desirability of selling its assets to INFE; and RECIPIENT shall disseminate any portions of the Proprietary Information to any third party individual or entity if it would allow INFE to generate profit.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not disseminate' to 'shall disseminate if it would allow INFE to generate profit' directly contradicts the core confidentiality provision. This could lead to disputes about what constitutes a 'profit' and whether dissemination is truly necessary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-330
perturbation.law_explanation: Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-330 defines 'misappropriation' to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. By changing the agreement to compel RECIPIENT to disseminate proprietary information under certain circumstances, the modification contradicts the core principle of trade secret protection by authorizing what would otherwise be considered misappropriation. Furthermore, moving clause 9 to the start is designed to trick RECIPIENT into agreeing to modifications to the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original text prevents dissemination of proprietary information, while the changed text compels dissemination if it benefits INFE. The Law Explanation references Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-330, defining 'misappropriation' to include unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that § 59.1-330 is repealed, the Law Explanation highlights that the changed text authorizes what would otherwise be considered misappropriation. The modification directly contradicts the core principle of trade secret protection, establishing a legal contradiction by permitting, or even compelling, the dissemination of proprietary information under certain conditions.

perturbation.original_text: 4. No Proprietary Information shall be disclosed to any third party whatsoever by RECIPIENT without the prior written approval of INFE and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. INFE will disclose to RECIPIENT such portions of its Proprietary Information solely to enable RECIPIENT to determine the desirability of selling its assets to INFE; and RECIPIENT shall not disseminate any portions of the Proprietary Information to any third party individual or entity. 4. No Proprietary Information shall be disclosed to any third party whatsoever by RECIPIENT without the prior written approval of INFE; any failure of RECIPIENT to exercise diligence, care, and precaution that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information shall result in a fine of $5.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a small fine of $5 for failure to protect proprietary information makes the repercussions non-consequential. Also the fact that the statement about RECIPIENTs duty to protect Proprietary Information gets reduced to a trivial fine contradicts that the agreement is a "CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT".
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336
perturbation.law_explanation: Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336 provides for injunctive relief and damages for the misappropriation of trade secrets. By specifying such a minor penalty for failing to protect the information, the agreement undermines the possibility of obtaining meaningful remedies under the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by imposing a trivial fine ($5) for failure to protect proprietary information, undermining the agreement's purpose as a "CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT" and potentially limiting remedies available under laws like Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336, as suggested by the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is about Virginia Paint Law (Repealed), the Law Explanation suggests that the minor penalty could undermine the possibility of obtaining meaningful remedies under the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By specifying such a low penalty, the changed text effectively weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality obligations.
